author: @JohnBoen
text:
This ties righr in with a project ai am building. This should work really well in a heat pipe.

That's a sealed coper tube containing a small amount of some volatile fluid - the heat is transfered rapidly via phase change of the fluid.  

Nanoparticles increase the thermal conductivity of the fluid and act as nucleation sites for the formation of bubbles.  Seems literally perfect for the job.

Fortunately, I learned how to make copper nanoparticles just last week.

Love this channel :)

author: @skaltura
text:
There's a mistake on testing methodology.
Since heat is IR radiation, black is going to collect that radiation extremely well, where as clear solution (water) will not collect barely any at all.
We also know that black of all colors is the one to most easily collect and release that heat radiation.

Because you are heating with a bulb not touching the liquid, you are only left with radiation to transfer the heat, and no convection what-so-ever.

I understand this is for solar panels, but are you going to be using clear tube for the graphene fluid to be exposed directly to sun in both cases? Nope, common sense tells not to.
What would be really interesting is to see the convection rate at different solutions in steps of few % from 0% to say 30%.

But we already also know that Graphene is the best conductor of heat known to man, so the question remains how well it is transferred through the medium (water and dilutant in this case).

EDIT: Yes i did comment before watching full video. The solution to get comparative test would have been using a bit of black colorant btw. I guess food colorant would be cheap option and only require a tiny bit :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +skaltura go right ahead and do it mate - i would love to see your results

author: @RickGWybou
text:
Robert, First off, thank you SO MUCH for publishing all these amazingly educational and inspirational science videos, focusing on simple and relatively inexpensive approaches to doing cutting edge science and technology.  I happened on one of our videos by accident, researching Stirling engines a few days ago, and I've been kept extremely busy, as well as entertained, ever since watching your other videos. 

In this video you talk about the enhanced thermal absorption properties of your graphene solution over tap water.  I was thinking this would enhance the efficiency of a passive solar water heater, which I've been designing.  There are many designs for DIY solar water heaters online, but I am trying to work within two constraints, one self imposed, the other environmentally imposed. 

The environmentally imposed constraint is that I live in Hamilton, Ontario Canada, so during the winter months the outside temperature will often drop well below freezing so whatever fluid I use in my pipes must include some kind of safe antifreeze.  My research into environmentally safe antifreeze products led me to ACTA's patented propylene glycol/water mixture which also, incidentally, increases heat transfer.  Do you think there is a way to combine your Graphene in water solution with a propylene glycol/water solution to form an even more efficient heat transfer liquid that will not freeze?

The self imposed constraint I'm trying to work with is that I want to minimize the size of the passive solar water heater I'm constructing as most of the online DIY designs are rather massive and not aesthetically appealing.  The more efficient the heat transfer, the more compact a unit can be.   

My idea is to dual purpose a south facing front porch railing - think wrought iron fence meets passive solar water heater.  Instead of wrought iron bars, I envision black painted copper pipes filled with an efficient heat transfer fluid enclosed in an air tight frame covered on both sides with glass or Plexiglas panels. To further boost the sun capture, I might add curved (preferably parabolic) troughs behind the pipes maximize the surface area of the pipe being struck by light. 

Both panels of glass  of this solar water heater could be coated with your transparent solar cell solution to turn them into a giant (90cm x 244cm) transparent solar cells. Please point me to which of your videos related to this topic offers the most promising power efficiency option for a transparent solar cell.

Thanks  again, for everything.

author: @dovkruger6242
text: Graphene is obviously useful for many things, but in this case, you haven't shown that it has any benefits over graphite, or even some kind of benign dye.  All chemisty is nanoscale, so before concluding that Graphene is the answer here, try a bit of food coloring or some other dye and compare it to graphene.  I would bet that in terms of collecting light, any dark liquid will perform roughly similarly.  Not sure about thermal transfer.

replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
	 	 	 		 	 	 	
So in principle your non waterproof graphene ink (which I can get more of) with a simple surfactant (I have some sea weed extracted for making super low surface tension solutions) would suffice. It would seem that the concentration is still subject to experimentation however from what your post suggests not very high.
This is a must, especially as I am still in the process of building the system.....Just to decide if it will be a conventional copper tube collector or try a low pressure glass tube collector.
Yes sadly you do have to be careful however unless things have changed and this is a blend, if you produce a standard product safety data sheet ( MSDS) you should be covered assuming there is no risk of a new compound being present.

Once again thanks for this post and exciting development.

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith In PC type cooling one is almost always best using a heat pipe arrangement.  Where the graphene would excel is in cases where you have a liquid with high heat capacity, but you have not the temperature difference for phase change.  Or maybe you don't want that high a temperature difference but still want high thermal transfer.  There are many applications where this is a desired feature.  Industrial Chemistry for one.  And yes geothermal loops would benefit, if you are pumping, but in such a case you would prefer heat pipes or thermosiphons.  Otec, chemical filtration, separation, purification are all use cases.  This is a case where if you can you patent for several applications.  For industrial use in chemical processing.  Desalination and OTEC are big winners.  As a thermal battery with water, as the water has high heat capacity and when stored at near ambient you can insulate and store thermal energy efficiently, like a capacitor, but with the added graphene you created a thermal super capacitor.  Like the LOW ESR in super capacitors, you have now lowered the barrier to getting the energy out, with small temperature differences. So the next step is insulating LTD stirling engines, and having two thermal batteries in which one switches the hot and cold to the thermal batteries.  If one uses high pressure but long slow strokes, then one can build something that will allow you to generate power 24/7 365 days a year from ambient temperature. Yes it will be large for the power output, but it also frees you up in some ways to use inexpensive materials such the only downside is the volume.  If One wishes to go further even then one can have the stirling engine like the balloon but use VHB 4910 or create your own (google is your friend)  and as dielectric material expands it has greater capacitance, and piezothread embedded generate the charge as they are being stressed. Look at Advanced Cerametrics Patent on piezoelectric threads. Its spun like Nylon that is how he got the idea.    When the gas is cooled down we have discharge into regular super capacitors.  This is good if you wish to go quick but I think going slow opens many more possibilities.  You build in the constraints and nature does the rest.

author: @TheGEODEAHOLIC
text: VERY interesting. I bet it wood B handy for car radiators, sterling engine water jackets, & hv ac & heat pump systems.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: definitely mate

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text: Thing is if you want to make geothermal as efficient as possible, then you want to reduce the energy pumping.  One way is heat pipes, the other is taking advantage of density and temperature difference like in the ocean thermoclines. The graphene enhanced fluid could enhance that and thermal diffusion within the columns of water, therefore its pumping itself although slowly, add some heat pipes to these differences and you have a small temperature difference which makes the heat pump more efficient, and the heat supplied to it is more efficient as it does the pumping itself based upon the density at temperature.  In such a case you could avoid even the drilling costs and have vertical pipes which are insulated and placed vertically with supports and even input with passive solar.  If you are digging through sand then by all means go into the ground but you have options.

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text:
Kimberly Peacock http://sensor.materials.drexel.edu/wp-content/uploads/2012/07/Graphene-lead-zirconate-titanate-optothermal-FET.pdf

I saw someone mention a TEG, induce cavitation and do this within a micro heat pipe array using pyroelectric sensors and you can convert up to 90% of the thermal flow as opposed to less than 10% with a conventional TEG.

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text:
Byproduct perfection!
Level up achieved.

But in all seriosity, as long as it works, and it works better than what's usually _on tap_ (literally in this case), it really doesn't matter where it comes from.
I'd hazard a guess that most of the bestest things evah were discovered rather than invented.

I assume this stuff would have a very slightly abrasive action in a pumped system, but slight enough for perhaps someone with a generally water cooled PC to try swapping the liquid out for this.
Most people with less than average PCs will have software for monitoring temperature(s) installed, and if _"good god!"_ is anything to go by, it might allow higher clock rates with almost no extra overhead.

Personally, I prefer the idea of passive systems so that possible mild abrasive action shouldn't ever be a bother to me.
Serendipitously, this may be exactly the edge I was going to be lacking in order to build the passive cooling system I have in mind.
Don't expect any feedback for a while though, I'm in the "R" part of what will be laborious "R&D" right now.
